name: skills-use-tools-not-implement-them
tags: skill design, mcp tools, task-implementation, separation of concerns, code vs skill, simple tools, avoid complex code, execution contract, north star
body:
Skills are prose orchestration over existing tools — they don't grow new mechanics themselves. If a skill's behavior needs a capability that doesn't exist yet, that capability gets built as a small, simple MCP tool/hook in code first; the skill then just calls it. Complexity belongs in the skill's judgment (prose), not in the tool's code — if a proposed tool is getting complex, that's a signal the design is wrong (either the tool is trying to make a judgment call that should stay in the skill, or the step should be broken into smaller, simpler tools).

A static, non-branching injection is the acceptable exception, not a violation: task:380140fa's Execution Contract (a fixed north-star string injected verbatim at task activation, same every turn, no per-turn logic) is simple code — write-once, read-verbatim — and stayed in the LangGraph/checkpoint layer rather than becoming skill prose. What made 2f0fb672's *other* pieces (per-tool Reflection, mechanical loop-detector) wrong to build in code wasn't "it's code" — it was that they required ongoing branching judgment (what changed, is this a loop, what's next) turn over turn. The dividing line is static-and-mechanical (fine as small code) vs. judgment-that-changes-with-context (belongs in skill prose or the model's own reasoning, not hardcoded).

Why: mirrors the reason 2f0fb672 was abandoned ([[behavioral-steering-lives-in-skills-not-langgraph]]) — mixing "new mechanism" work into skill prose makes the skill do two jobs (define behavior + invent plumbing), and it makes gaps hard to spot (a skill step that silently can't execute because the tool it assumes doesn't exist). Symmetrically, letting a tool grow complex to absorb judgment that belongs in the skill produces the same tangling in the other direction — brittle code standing in for prose that should have stayed prose.

How to apply: when drafting a new skill (e.g. task-implementation) or expanding an existing one, first check which steps need a tool that doesn't exist — build/extend those in code (dispatcher.py, src/tools/*.py) as their own task, then write the skill assuming the tool is already there. Keep each such tool small and mechanical (fetch, check, write one thing, or inject a fixed static string) — if implementing it starts requiring branching judgment logic that varies turn-to-turn, push that judgment back into the skill's prose instead and have the tool just expose the raw signal. Don't describe a step in skill prose that depends on a tool not yet built without flagging it as a prerequisite.
